name: Balor
description: Balor, also known as Balor of the Evil Eye, is a prominent figure in Irish mythology, particularly within the Ulster Cycle and the Mythological Cycle. He is depicted as a powerful and malevolent giant, often associated with death, blight, and destruction. Balor was the king of the Fomorians, a race of supernatural beings who were frequently at odds with the Tuatha Dé Danann, the gods of Ireland. His most distinguishing feature was his single, immense eye, which possessed a deadly gaze capable of instantly killing or withering anything it looked upon. This eye was typically kept closed, requiring multiple attendants to lift the eyelid when its destructive power was needed. Balor's reign was one of terror, and his eventual demise came at the hands of his grandson, Lugh, during the Second Battle of Mag Tuired. The story of Balor serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers of unchecked power and the cyclical nature of fate.
subtitle: King of the Fomorians with a deadly eye.
appearance: Balor is typically described as a towering giant with a single, massive eye in the center of his forehead. His appearance is often fearsome and grotesque, reflecting his malevolent nature. Some accounts depict him as having a wild beard and unkempt hair, further emphasizing his untamed and destructive force.
habitat: Balor resided in fortresses or strongholds within the Fomorian territories, often located in remote and desolate areas, such as islands or rugged coastlines. These locations reflected the Fomorians' association with chaos and the untamed aspects of nature.
behavior: Balor is characterized by his tyrannical and ruthless behavior. He is depicted as a cruel and oppressive ruler, feared by his own people and despised by the Tuatha Dé Danann. He is driven by a lust for power and a desire to dominate all of Ireland.
weaknesses: His own eye, if reflected back at him
Prophecy of being killed by his grandson

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Balor's Eye
keyMyths.description: Balor's deadly eye was not innate but acquired through an accident. While spying on his father's druids, who were brewing a potent potion of evil, the fumes entered his eye, imbuing it with its destructive power. This origin story highlights the accidental nature of his power and the unintended consequences of meddling with dark forces.
keyMyths.source: Lebor na hUidre (Book of the Dun Cow)

keyMyths.title: The Second Battle of Mag Tuired
keyMyths.description: Balor played a crucial role in the Second Battle of Mag Tuired, where the Fomorians clashed with the Tuatha Dé Danann. His deadly gaze wreaked havoc on the Tuatha Dé Danann's forces until Lugh, his grandson, managed to strike him in the eye with a sling stone, reversing its power and killing Balor instantly. This marked a turning point in the battle and a victory for the Tuatha Dé Danann.
keyMyths.source: Cath Maige Tuired (The Second Battle of Mag Tuired)

keyMyths.title: The Prophecy of Balor's Death
keyMyths.description: A prophecy foretold that Balor would be killed by his own grandson. To prevent this, he imprisoned his only daughter, Ethniu, to prevent her from having children. However, Cian of the Tuatha Dé Danann managed to reach Ethniu, and she bore Lugh, fulfilling the prophecy despite Balor's efforts. This highlights the theme of fate and the futility of trying to escape one's destiny.

sources.author: Peter Berresford Ellis
sources.description: A comprehensive dictionary covering various aspects of Irish mythology, including detailed entries on Balor and the Fomorians.
sources.text: A comprehensive dictionary covering various aspects of Irish mythology, including detailed entries on Balor and the Fomorians.

sources.author: John Arnott MacCulloch
sources.work: The Religion of the Ancient Celts
sources.description: An analysis of the religious beliefs and practices of the ancient Celts, providing context for understanding the role of figures like Balor in their mythology.
